g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of ARM Design Philosophy. Let us discover how this architecture revolutionized computing.
ARM (Advanced RISC Machines) is the dominant processor architecture powering over 95% of smartphones and billions of IoT devices worldwide.
g.text: ARM (Advanced RISC Machines) is a family of RISC processor architectures known for
power efficiency. It powers 95%+ of smartphones, billions of IoT devices,
and is expanding into servers and laptops. Originally developed by Acorn in 1985.

g.data-narration: ARM follows RISC principles with pragmatic adaptations: simple instruction set, load-store architecture, and power-efficient design for embedded applications.
In ARM's load-store architecture, arithmetic operations occur ONLY between registers. Memory access requires explicit LOAD or STORE instructions.
g.text: Arithmetic operations occur ONLY between registers. Memory access requires explicit LOAD/STORE.

g.data-narration: ARM's unique conditional execution feature allows most instructions to execute based on CPSR flags, eliminating branches for simple conditions.
g.text: Most instructions can execute conditionally based on CPSR flags, eliminating branches.
ADDEQ R2, R3, R4 ; Add if equal
SUBNE R2, R3, R4 ; Sub if not equal

g.data-narration: ARM's barrel shifter allows shift operations combined with arithmetic in single-cycle instructions, enabling efficient multiplication by powers of 2.
g.text: Shift operations can be combined with arithmetic/logical operations in a single cycle.

g.data-narration: Thumb is a 16-bit compressed instruction set providing 65% code size of ARM while maintaining 60-70% performance - crucial for memory-constrained embedded systems.
g.text: 16-bit compressed instructions for better code density in memory-constrained embedded applications.
Thumb achieves ~65% of ARM code size

g.data-narration: Thumb-2 combines 16-bit and 32-bit instructions, achieving 98% ARM performance with Thumb code density - now primary for Cortex-M processors.
g.text: Hybrid approach combining 16-bit and 32-bit instructions for optimal efficiency.

g.data-narration: ARM pipelines evolved from 3-stage (ARM7) to 15-stage (Cortex-A15). Deeper pipelines enable higher clock speeds but increase branch penalty.
g.text: Pipeline depth affects clock speed and branch penalty. Deeper pipelines = higher speeds but more complex.

g.data-narration: ARM supports 7 execution modes with banked registers for fast context switching: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, and System.
g.text: Seven execution modes with banked registers enable fast context switching during exceptions.
Each mode has dedicated SP, LR, SPSR
FIQ has its own R8-R12 for fast interrupt handling

g.data-narration: The CPSR (Current Program Status Register) contains condition flags N, Z, C, V that control conditional execution and indicate arithmetic results.
g.text: Contains condition flags, processor mode bits, and interrupt disable bits.

g.data-narration: ARM architecture evolved from ARMv4 (first 32-bit) to ARMv8 (64-bit AArch64). Each version added DSP instructions, SIMD, and security features.
g.text: From ARMv4 to ARMv8, each version added significant capabilities.

g.data-narration: ARM Cortex processors are segmented: Cortex-M for microcontrollers, Cortex-R for real-time, and Cortex-A for high-performance applications.
g.text: Three distinct families optimized for different market segments.

g.data-narration: Practical examples demonstrate ARM efficiency: conditional execution eliminates branches, barrel shifter enables single-cycle multiplication.
g.text: CMP R0,R1 / ADDEQ R2,R3,R4 / SUBNE R2,R3,R4

g.data-narration: In summary, ARM's pragmatic RISC design with conditional execution, barrel shifting, Thumb-2, and efficient power management makes it ideal for embedded systems.
